name: Abingdon
intro: Abingdon sits on the River Thames six miles south of Oxford, one of the oldest towns in England. Gardens here range from compact period courtyards near the town centre to larger family homes on the estates at Peachcroft and Fitzharris. The soil is mostly river gravel and alluvium, free-draining and fertile, which suits a wide range of plants. The town's position in the Thames floodplain means some gardens near the river can be waterlogged in winter.
soil_type: Free-draining sandy loam on gravel terraces. Heavier clay loam on the higher ground south of the town.
soil_ph: Neutral to slightly alkaline, pH 6.5 to 7.5. Free-draining gravel near the river, heavier clay on the higher ground.
drainage: River gravels and alluvium on the Thames floodplain, with Oxford Clay on higher ground to the south.
rainfall: Approximately 650mm per year, one of the drier parts of Oxfordshire.
garden_type: Period townhouses near the centre, 1930s semis on the outer estates, modern developments at Peachcroft and Fitzharris, rural properties on the edge of the Thames floodplain.
local_conditions: Free-draining gravel suits most plants. Riverside gardens can be waterlogged in winter. Frost pockets near the river can damage early growth.
temperature: Annual average 11C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C. Frost pockets near the river.

name: Amersham
intro: Amersham sits in a valley in the Chilterns, with the historic Old Town in the valley bottom and newer development climbing the surrounding hills. The town sits on chalk, with clay-with-flints on the hilltops. Many properties have steep sloping gardens that need careful terracing and planting.
soil_type: Shallow chalky loam in the valley. Heavy clay-loam with flints on the hills.
soil_ph: Alkaline in the valley, pH 7.5 to 8.0. Neutral to slightly acidic on the hills, pH 6.5 to 7.0.
drainage: Chalk in the valley bottom, clay-with-flints on the hillsides and hilltops.
rainfall: Approximately 720mm per year. Slightly wetter than the surrounding area.
garden_type: Historic cottages and townhouses in Old Amersham, larger family homes on the hills, modern developments around Amersham-on-the-Hill.
local_conditions: Steep slopes, frost pockets in the valley, mature beechwoods nearby providing leaf mould for mulch.
temperature: Annual average 10.3C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost pockets in the valley.

name: Ascot
intro: Ascot is famous for its racecourse, but the town and surrounding area also have some of the most sought-after gardens in Berkshire. Properties around Ascot, Sunninghill and South Ascot range from substantial family homes to large estate gardens. The soil is mostly acidic sand and gravel, which suits acid-loving plants like rhododendrons and camellias, but presents challenges for plants that prefer alkaline conditions.
soil_type: Free-draining sandy loam on the Bagshot Formation. Heavier clay in pockets where London Clay shows through.
soil_ph: Acidic, pH 5.0 to 6.5. Ideal for rhododendrons, camellias and azaleas.
drainage: Bagshot Formation sand and gravel, with London Clay beneath. Acidic, free-draining soil.
garden_type: Substantial family homes, large detached properties, estate gardens, modern developments. Gardens tend to be large and well-established.
local_conditions: Acidic free-draining soil suits acid-loving plants. Drought can be an issue in summer on sandy soil. Mature trees provide shade but compete for moisture.
temperature: Annual average 11C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C. Mild microclimate due to urban heat island effect.

name: Aylesbury
intro: Aylesbury sits in the flat clay vale between the Chilterns and the Northamptonshire uplands. The soil here is heavy clay, very different from the chalk a few miles south. Gardens need careful drainage work and the choice of plants has to suit moisture-retentive clay rather than free-draining chalk.
soil_type: Heavy clay that holds water in winter and cracks in summer. Some gravel terraces near the river.
soil_ph: Slightly alkaline, pH 7.0 to 7.8. Clay soils with high lime content in places.
drainage: Oxford Clay and Gault Clay, with gravel terraces along the River Thame.
rainfall: Approximately 640 to 680mm per year. One of the drier parts of southern England.
garden_type: Mix of post-war council housing with generous back gardens, modern developments at Quarrendon and Weedon Hill, and rural properties on the edge of the vale.
local_conditions: Heavy clay holds water through winter. Drainage is the main challenge. Some new-build gardens have compacted subsoil from construction.
temperature: Annual average 10.8C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C. Frost pockets in low-lying gardens.

name: Bicester
intro: Bicester is a growing market town in north-east Oxfordshire, sitting on the heavy Oxford Clay that characterises this part of the vale. The town has expanded rapidly with new developments at Kingsmere and Elmsbrook, but older properties around the market square retain established gardens. Heavy clay soil dominates, holding water through winter and cracking in dry summers. The town's position on the flat vale means frost pockets are common in low-lying gardens on cold, still nights. Gardeners here need to choose plants that tolerate wet feet in winter and improve soil structure with regular additions of organic matter. Many of the new-build estates have compacted subsoil that needs serious improvement before planting can thrive.
soil_type: Heavy clay that holds water in winter and cracks in summer. Some gravel terraces near the brook.
soil_ph: Slightly alkaline, pH 7.0 to 7.8 on the Oxford Clay. Higher pH where limestone shows through.
drainage: Oxford Clay bedrock with gravel terraces along the River Bure and Bicester Brook. Some Cornbrash limestone to the east.
rainfall: Approximately 640mm per year. One of the drier parts of Oxfordshire.
garden_type: Mix of period properties around the market square, 1970s housing estates at Southwold, and modern developments at Kingsmere and Elmsbrook.
local_conditions: Heavy clay holds water through winter. Drainage is the main challenge. New-build gardens often have compacted subsoil from construction.
temperature: Annual average 10.7C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 1C. Frost pockets in low-lying gardens on the vale.

name: Bracknell
intro: Bracknell is a Berkshire new town surrounded by the pine woods and heathland of the Bagshot Sands. The town was substantially rebuilt in the 1950s and 1960s, with new housing estates at Birch Hill, Great Hollands and Hanworth laid out with generous garden plots. The sandy acidic soil is very different from the chalk of the nearby Chilterns, suiting ericaceous plants and woodland gardens rather than Mediterranean favourites. Many properties have generous gardens thanks to the new town layout, with mature conifers and pines providing structure and shelter. The surrounding Crown Estate woodlands give the town a green character with extensive tree cover that shades many gardens. Sandy soil drains freely but needs regular mulching and feeding to maintain fertility through the growing season.

soil_ph: Acidic, pH 5.5 to 6.5. Suitable for ericaceous plants such as camellias and rhododendrons.
drainage: Bagshot Beds sand with Plateau Gravel caps. London Clay at depth.
rainfall: Approximately 680mm per year. Wetter than nearby Wokingham due to elevation.
garden_type: 1950s and 1960s new town housing with generous gardens, modern developments at The Lexicon and Jennett's Park, family homes at Warfield and Binfield.
local_conditions: Acidic sandy soil needs regular mulching. Pine trees and conifers common. Woodland shade in many gardens.
temperature: Annual average 10.5C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost in low-lying gardens.

name: Chesham
intro: Chesham sits in a steep valley at the end of the Metropolitan Line, with the River Chess running through the town centre. The town has a long industrial history and a mix of period cottages and modern estates climbing the surrounding slopes. The valley bottom sits on chalk, while the slopes above have clay-with-flints, giving variable soil conditions across the town over short distances. Frost pockets form in the valley bottom, catching cold air from the surrounding hills and causing late spring damage to tender plants. Many gardens are small but productive, with traditional cottage styles still common in the older parts of town. Slope and aspect matter as much as soil type when choosing plants here.

name: Chinnor
intro: Chinnor sits at the foot of the Chiltern escarpment in south Oxfordshire, with the steep chalk slopes rising dramatically behind the village. The village has a long history of chalk extraction, with the former cement works having shaped the local landscape and economy. Chalky soil dominates, draining freely and warming quickly in spring, which gives gardeners a head start on the season. Many properties have views across the Vale of Aylesbury and established gardens that benefit from the free-draining soil. The Ridgeway footpath runs along the hilltops above the village, bringing walkers and exposing the area's chalk downland character. Hillside plots above the village need careful terracing and drought-tolerant planting to cope with shallow soil and exposed conditions.
soil_type: Shallow free-draining chalky loam with flint. Deeper soils in the village centre where soil has accumulated.
soil_ph: Alkaline, pH 7.5 to 8.2 on the chalk.

temperature: Annual average 10.3C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost pockets at the foot of the escarpment.

name: Gerrards Cross
intro: Gerrards Cross is an affluent commuter village in south Buckinghamshire, sitting on the clay-with-flints that caps the Chiltern hills. The town has substantial family homes with large established gardens, many set on generous plots with mature trees and specimen shrubs. name: Thame
intro: Thame is an historic market town on the River Thame, with a wide High Street and a famous cattle market that ran for centuries. The town sits on heavy clay with limestone bands beneath, which gives gardens variable soil conditions across different streets. Many properties have substantial rear gardens, particularly along the older streets and out towards Moreton. The town's position at the foot of the Chilterns means it is slightly drier than the heights above, but cold air still pools in the meadows around the river. Gardeners here work with moisture-retentive clay that needs careful plant selection and regular soil improvement to thrive.
soil_type: Heavy clay with limestone fragments. Rich alluvial soil near the river.
soil_ph: Slightly alkaline, pH 7.0 to 7.8. The Cornbrash limestone lifts pH in places.
drainage: Oxford Clay with Cornbrash limestone bands. River gravels and alluvium along the River Thame floodplain.
rainfall: Approximately 650mm per year. Drier than the Chilterns to the south.
garden_type: Period townhouses along the High Street with walled rear gardens, family homes at Towersey Road and Chinnor Road, modern estates at Thame Park.
local_conditions: Clay soil needs improvement for growing. Riverside meadows can flood in winter. Mature trees shade many older gardens.
temperature: Annual average 10.6C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost along the river meadows.

name: Thatcham
intro: Thatcham sits on the River Kennet east of Newbury, with a mix of period properties and modern developments. The soil is mostly chalk and gravel on the higher ground, with alluvial soil in the valley. The area is known for its nature reserves and the Reedbeds along the Kennet.
soil_type: Free-draining chalky loam on the higher ground. Rich alluvial soil in the valley.
soil_ph: Alkaline on the chalk, pH 7.5 to 8.0. Neutral in the valley.
drainage: Upper Chalk on the higher ground, river gravel and alluvium in the Kennet valley.
garden_type: Period properties in the old town, modern developments, rural properties towards the river.
local_conditions: Chalky soil suits Mediterranean plants. Valley gardens can flood. Frost pockets near the river.
temperature: Annual average 10.5C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C.

soil_ph: Neutral to slightly acidic, pH 6.0 to 7.0.
drainage: River gravels and alluvium on the Thames floodplain, with London Clay beneath.
garden_type: Period properties near the centre, family homes, modern developments. Gardens tend to be medium-sized.
local_conditions: Gravel soils drain freely but can be drought-prone in summer. Riverside gardens can flood in winter. Frost pockets near the river.
temperature: Annual average 10.8C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C.

name: Watlington
intro: Watlington is a small market town at the foot of the Chiltern escarpment, with Watlington Hill rising steeply to the east. The town has Norman origins and a distinctive triangular Market Square that gives it a strong sense of place. Chalky soil dominates, with the free-draining conditions suiting Mediterranean planting and traditional cottage garden styles. Many properties have small walled gardens, while larger homes on the outskirts have more substantial plots with mature trees. The town sits at the eastern edge of the Oxfordshire chalk country, where the hills meet the clay of the Vale. Exposed hillside gardens face wind and need shelter belts, while valley plots suffer from cold air pooling on still nights.
soil_type: Shallow free-draining chalky loam with flint. Deeper alluvial soils along the chalk streams.
soil_ph: Alkaline, pH 7.5 to 8.2 on the chalk.
drainage: Upper Chalk at the foot of Watlington Hill, with clay-with-flints capping the heights. Chalk streams run along the valley floors.
garden_type: Period cottages around the Market Square with small walled gardens, family homes on the slopes, larger properties on the edge of town.
local_conditions: Shallow chalk soil needs careful plant choice. Exposed hillside gardens face wind. Mature yew and beech hedges common.
key_services: Hedge cutting for yew and beech
temperature: Annual average 10.4C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost pockets at the foot of the escarpment.

name: Wendover
intro: Wendover sits at the foot of the Chiltern escarpment, where the Grand Union Canal threads through the town and beechwoods rise steeply to the south. The town has a long market history and many period properties with established cottage gardens along the High Street. Chalky soil dominates, draining freely and suiting Mediterranean herbs, lavender and roses. Frost pockets form in the valley bottom, which catches cold air draining off the surrounding hills in winter. Wendover Woods and Coombe Hill sit just above the town, providing leaf mould and a useful source of local mulch material. The combination of steep slopes, chalky subsoil and cold valley air makes plant selection critical for successful gardens here.
soil_type: Shallow free-draining chalky loam with flint. Deeper alluvial soil near the canal and streams.
soil_ph: Alkaline, pH 7.5 to 8.2 on the chalk. Neutral to slightly acidic where clay-with-flints caps the hills.
drainage: Upper Chalk at the foot of the escarpment, with clay-with-flints on the heights above. The Wendover Arm of the Grand Union Canal cuts through chalk bedrock.
rainfall: Approximately 720mm per year. Slightly wetter than the vale to the north.
garden_type: Period cottages along the High Street with small walled gardens, larger family homes on the slopes, and rural properties towards Wendover Woods.
local_conditions: Frost pockets in the valley bottom, shallow chalk soil, mature beech and yew hedges.
key_services: Hedge cutting for beech and yew
temperature: Annual average 10.2C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost likely from November to April, with sharp frost pockets in the valley.

name: Windsor
intro: Windsor is a historic royal town on the banks of the Thames, dominated by Windsor Castle and surrounded by the Great Park. The town sits on Thames river gravels, which give free-draining soil suitable for a wide range of plants including many traditional English favourites. Period properties along the High Street and in Eton have small courtyard gardens, while larger Victorian and Edwardian villas sit on the higher ground at Clewer and St Leonards. The Great Park provides a backdrop of mature trees and ornamental planting that influences garden styles across the town. The town's relatively mild climate and fertile soil make it a rewarding area for gardening, with less frost than the surrounding countryside. Riverside gardens near Datchet and Old Windsor may have waterlogging issues in wet winters despite the free-draining gravel.
soil_type: Free-draining gravelly loam. Rich alluvial soil near the river.
soil_ph: Slightly alkaline to neutral, pH 6.8 to 7.5. Alkaline where chalky gravel caps the clay.
drainage: Thames river gravels and alluvium. Reading Formation and London Clay at depth.
rainfall: Approximately 640mm per year. One of the drier parts of Berkshire.
garden_type: Period townhouses in the town centre with courtyard gardens, larger villas on the heights, modern developments at Clewer and Dedworth.
local_conditions: Free-draining gravelly soil suits many plants. Riverside gardens can be waterlogged in winter. Mature trees shade many properties.
key_services: Hedge cutting for yew and box
temperature: Annual average 11C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C. Frost in riverside gardens.

name: Woburn
intro: Woburn is a small Bedfordshire town built around the Duke of Bedford's Woburn Abbey estate, which gives it a distinctive architectural and horticultural character. The town sits on the Lower Greensand ridge, which gives it a distinctive sandy free-draining soil very different from the heavy clay of the surrounding vale. The Abbey's extensive gardens and deer park have shaped local horticultural traditions for centuries and still influence planting styles today. Many properties in the town are estate cottages with characterful established gardens that benefit from the light, workable soil. The sandy soil warms quickly in spring but needs regular feeding and mulching to maintain fertility through the growing season. Acidic conditions here suit ericaceous plants such as camellias, rhododendrons and blueberries better than anywhere else in our service area.
soil_type: Free-draining sandy loam over greensand. Easy to work but needs regular organic matter.
soil_ph: Slightly acidic, pH 6.0 to 6.8. The sandy greensand is naturally acidic.
drainage: Lower Greensand with ironstone bands. Chalk to the south, Oxford Clay to the north.
rainfall: Approximately 620mm per year. One of the drier parts of Bedfordshire.
garden_type: Estate cottages along the High Street with small front gardens, larger family homes on the approaches, modern developments at the edges of the town.
local_conditions: Sandy soil drains freely and is easy to work. Needs regular mulching. Acidic conditions suit ericaceous plants.
temperature: Annual average 10.5C. Summer highs 22C. Winter lows 1C. Frost pockets in low-lying gardens.

name: Wokingham
intro: Wokingham is an historic market town in Berkshire, sitting on the boundary between London Clay and the Bagshot Beds. The town has expanded significantly with modern developments at Norreys and Emmbrook, but the town centre retains period properties with established gardens. Soil conditions vary across the town, from heavy clay on lower ground to lighter sandy soil on the Bagshot Beds to the south at Finchampstead. Many gardens have mature trees from the town's Victorian and Edwardian development that provide shade but compete for moisture and nutrients. The town's position in the Thames Valley gives it a relatively mild climate with less frost than the surrounding higher ground. New-build plots at Woosehill and elsewhere often have compacted subsoil that needs serious improvement before planting.
soil_type: Heavy clay on London Clay. Lighter sandy loam on the Bagshot Beds. Variable across the town.
soil_ph: Slightly acidic to neutral, pH 6.0 to 7.0. More acidic on the Bagshot Beds.
drainage: London Clay to the north, Bagshot Beds sand to the south. River gravels along the Emm Brook.
rainfall: Approximately 660mm per year. Drier than the Chilterns to the north.
garden_type: Period townhouses around the Market Place with walled gardens, family homes at Emmbrook and Norreys, modern estates at Woosehill and Finchampstead.
local_conditions: Variable soil from clay to sand. Mature trees shade many gardens. Some new-build plots have compacted subsoil.
temperature: Annual average 10.8C. Summer highs 23C. Winter lows 2C. Frost in low-lying gardens.
